Hero.text: {=!}The twenty-ninth Mughal Emperor and the last to rule a nominally united Hindustan, Muhammad Shah inherited a crumbling empire and a court in open chaos. Known as Rangila, the Colourful, for his love of music, art, and poetry, he is not the warrior the moment demands. Yet behind the hedonism lies a canny survivor who has outlasted multiple coups and palace intrigues. He knows he cannot fight the Marathas, the Afghans, and his own Nawabs simultaneously, so he haggles, delays, and plays factions against each other, buying time for an empire whose time may already have passed.

{=!}The first independent Nawab of Bengal and its most capable administrator, Murshid Quli Khan built Bengal into the wealthiest province in the known world. A converted Hindu orphan raised at the Mughal court, his genius was for revenue collection and commercial policy. He restructured the zamindari system, imposed disciplined tax reforms, and drew the European trading companies into arrangements that made Bengal prosper without making it dependent on them. He rules the province as a private kingdom while paying nominal deference to Delhi. The fiction of Mughal suzerainty satisfies both parties.

{=!}Mir Qamar ud-Din Khan, titled Asaf Jah and Nizam ul-Mulk, is perhaps the most capable administrator alive in Hindostan. A veteran of four emperors' courts, he escaped the viper pit of Delhi to carve out the Deccan Plateau as his own domain. Methodical, cold-blooded, and patient, Asaf Jah combined Persian administrative traditions with locally recruited Deccani cavalry to build Hyderabad into a formidable state. He views the Maratha Confederacy as the primary existential threat and has organised his entire kingdom around containing it. He legitimizes his de facto independence by always bowing, very formally, toward Shahjahanabad.

{=!}Born of the Abdali clan of Pashtuns, Ahmad Shah rose through the ranks of the Persian imperial army to command its finest Afghan cavalry contingent. A natural horseman with a general's instinct, he united the fractious Pashtun tribes under his banner through equal measures of iron diplomacy and relentless force. His vision extends far beyond raiding the plains of India. He intends to build a lasting Durrani Sultanate that controls the great mountain passes between east and west, commanding the trade routes that have made conquerors rich since Alexander marched through Kandahar.

{=!}The ruling Maharaja of Mysore and heir to an ancient dynasty tracing its lineage to the medieval Yadava kings, Krishnaraja Wodeyar represents the sacred legitimacy of the Gaddi of Srirangapatna. A man of gentle disposition and genuine scholarly learning, his reign has been increasingly shaped by the military ambitions of his powerful Dalvai commanders. Under Krishnaraja, Mysore has expanded its revenue base, reformed its armies, and asserted independence from both the Mughal Deccan Sultanates and the Maratha Confederacy. He rules from the throne; his commanders have made that throne something others fear.

{=!}A soldier who rose from an ordinary trooper to Dalvai of Mysore by sheer talent, Hyder Ali cannot read or write, yet his grasp of logistics, terrain, and human nature is extraordinary. He rebuilt Mysore's infantry on European lines, hired French officers to train his gunners, and developed supply chains that allow his armies to campaign in terrain that would starve any other force. His strategic vision is decades ahead of his contemporaries: he understands that the coming wars will be won by economic organisation and disciplined musketry, not ancestral cavalry charges. Utterly self-made, he is the true architect of Mysore's power.

{=!}The Maharaja of Jaipur and paramount leader of the Rajputana Federation, Sawai Jai Singh is a man who contains multitudes. As a warrior, he commanded Mughal cavalry with distinction across the Deccan campaigns. As a scholar, he constructed five great astronomical observatories, the Jantar Mantars, and corresponded with European scientists as an intellectual equal. As a politician, he has navigated the collapse of Mughal authority with greater skill than any of his peers, using marriages, alliances, and patient negotiation where others reached for the sword. He knows the age of pure martial tradition is ending, and he is positioning Rajputana for the world that comes next.

{=!}The Peshwa of the Maratha Confederacy and its greatest battlefield commander, Bajirao I has never lost a battle. From his early twenties he has commanded the Maratha horse with a genius that is simultaneously terrifying and elegant, moving armies the way a chess master moves pieces, always three turns ahead. His strategy of Hindu Pad Padshahi, a Hindu sovereign empire of Hindostan, is both military objective and political creed. Of magnetic personal courage and extraordinary physical endurance, Bajirao represents a new kind of Indian leadership: not the inherited kingship of the Rajputs, but authority earned through unbroken victory and the loyalty of men who would follow him into the jaws of death.

{=!}Sirdar of the Sukerchakia Misl and one of the most formidable commanders of the Sikh Dal Khalsa, Charat Singh built his warband from a handful of horsemen into a force that dominates the Rechna Doab between the Chenab and Ravi rivers. He combines the Khalsa code's egalitarianism with a chieftain's ruthless pragmatism, surviving assassination attempts, imperial campaigns, and inter-Misl feuds to emerge as the dominant figure in the confederation. It is his bloodline, through his grandson Ranjit Singh, that will one day unite all the Misls into a single Sikh empire. Charat Singh himself is already, unknowingly, laying that foundation.

Hero.name: {=!}Aurangzeb Alamgir
Hero.text: {=!}Muhi-ud-Din Muhammad, who reigns as Aurangzeb Alamgir, is the sixth and last of the great Mughals — an austere, iron-willed zealot who pushed the empire to its largest extent and, in doing so, exhausted it. Half a century on the throne and a quarter-century of unending Deccan war have hollowed the treasury and the army both. Now, past ninety and failing in his camp in the Deccan, he knows what is coming: he has already divided the empire among his sons in his will, an arrangement none of them will honour. He dies as he lived, fearful for his soul and certain that the edifice he built will not outlast him.

Hero.name: {=!}Bahadur Shah I
Hero.text: {=!}Bahadur Shah I, born Muazzam, comes to the throne past sixty after defeating his brothers in the war of succession his father foresaw. A milder man than Aurangzeb, conciliatory toward Rajputs, Marathas and Sikhs alike, he tries to bind the empire's wounds with compromise where his father used the sword. But he is old, and the years left to him are few. The nobility has already learned the lesson of the succession war: that the throne is a prize to be seized, and that an emperor reigns only so long as the great Amirs permit it.

Hero.name: {=!}Jahandar Shah
Hero.text: {=!}Jahandar Shah takes the throne through the backing of Zulfiqar Khan, the kingmaker, and rules as that minister's puppet. Pleasure-loving and pliable, he is the first Mughal emperor to be openly made and unmade by his own nobles. His brief, dissolute reign scandalises the court and convinces every governor in Hindostan that the centre no longer holds. Within the year he will be betrayed, deposed, and strangled — a warning, and an invitation, to all who watch from the provinces.

Hero.name: {=!}Farrukhsiyar
Hero.text: {=!}Farrukhsiyar is raised to the throne by the Sayyid Brothers of Barha, the two soldier-statesmen whose armies now decide who wears the crown. Handsome, vain, and treacherous by turns, he conspires endlessly against the very kingmakers who made him, and they against him. It is during his reign that the English secure their fateful trading firman for Bengal. In the end the Sayyids tire of him: he is seized in the harem, blinded, and killed — the most brutal deposition yet, and proof that the office of Emperor has become a thing men kill for and discard.

Hero.name: {=!}Rafi ud-Darajat
Hero.text: {=!}Rafi ud-Darajat, a youth of eighteen, is lifted onto the throne by the Sayyid Brothers as a compliant figurehead the moment Farrukhsiyar is removed. He is already dying of consumption when he is crowned and is never more than a name read out in the Friday prayers. His reign is measured in weeks. He is set aside as quickly as he was raised, and death takes him soon after — a child-emperor whose whole reign was a formality of the kingmakers.

Hero.name: {=!}Shah Jahan II
Hero.text: {=!}Rafi ud-Daulah, proclaimed as Shah Jahan II, is the brother the Sayyid Brothers raise once the consumptive Rafi ud-Darajat fails them. He too is mortally ill, an opium-eater wasting before the court's eyes, and he too reigns only a few months before death — or the kingmakers' impatience — ends it. With his passing the Sayyids reach at last for a healthier instrument: a young prince named Muhammad, who will outlive them all and reign for thirty years over the wreck they leave behind.

Hero.name: {=!}Azim-ush-Shan
Hero.text: {=!}Azim-ush-Shan, second son of Bahadur Shah I and long the favourite to succeed him, was among the ablest of his generation — governor of Bengal, Bihar and Orissa, and rich enough to bankroll a bid for the throne. In the war of succession that followed his father's death he was outmanoeuvred and killed before he could be crowned. His son Farrukhsiyar would avenge him by taking the throne himself.

Hero.name: {=!}Rafi-ush-Shan
Hero.text: {=!}Rafi-ush-Shan, a son of Bahadur Shah I, perished with his brothers in the fratricidal war that opened after their father's death. He never reigned, but both of his sons — Rafi ud-Darajat and Rafi ud-Daulah — would briefly be raised to the throne as puppets of the Sayyid Brothers.

Hero.name: {=!}Jahan Shah
Hero.text: {=!}Jahan Shah, known also as Khujista Akhtar, was the youngest son of Bahadur Shah I and fell, like his brothers, in the succession war of 1712. He left a son, the young prince Muhammad, who would outlast every rival and reign for thirty years as Muhammad Shah.

Hero.name: {=!}Nawab Bai
Hero.text: {=!}Nawab Bai, a consort of Aurangzeb and mother of the prince Muazzam who would reign as Bahadur Shah I. A Kashmiri noblewoman of devout temperament, she spent much of her life apart from the austere emperor, her fortunes rising and falling with her son's standing at court.

Hero.name: {=!}Nizam Bai
Hero.text: {=!}Nizam Bai, principal consort of Bahadur Shah I and matriarch of the princes who contested the throne after him. Through her sons and their sons ran the rival claims that the Sayyid kingmakers would exploit for a decade.

Hero.name: {=!}Sahiba Niswan
Hero.text: {=!}Sahiba Niswan, consort of the prince Azim-ush-Shan and mother of Farrukhsiyar. When her husband fell in the succession war, it was her son who carried the family's claim to the Peacock Throne.

Hero.name: {=!}Nur-un-Nissa Begum
Hero.text: {=!}Nur-un-Nissa Begum, consort of the prince Rafi-ush-Shan and mother of the two brothers — Rafi ud-Darajat and Rafi ud-Daulah — whom the Sayyid Brothers would raise and discard within a single year.

Hero.name: {=!}Fakhr-un-Nissa Begum
Hero.text: {=!}Fakhr-un-Nissa Begum, consort of the prince Jahan Shah and mother of the young Muhammad, who would survive the slaughter of his uncles and reign for thirty years as Muhammad Shah.

{=!}Son of Hyder Ali and already acclaimed as a commander of rare brilliance, Tipu Sultan has mastered both traditional Nayaka cavalry tactics and modern musketry with a speed that astonishes veterans twice his age. His particular genius lies in rocket warfare: he has championed iron-cased rockets deployed in massed batteries as a weapon to break enemy formations before cavalry closes. Deeply religious and fiercely independent, Tipu understands what few other lords yet grasp: that the European trading companies arriving on India's coasts are not merely merchants, but the vanguard of something far more dangerous. He has made it his life's work to stop them.

{=!}Nanjarajayya of the Kalale leads the family that furnished Mysore its Dalvais — the hereditary war-ministers who commanded the Gaddi's armies while the Wodeyars reigned — until a trooper of genius named Hyder Ali rose through their own ranks and out-generalled them at their own trade. The Kalale keep their estates, their pride, and a very precise memory of everything they have lost; Nanjarajayya serves the state correctly and watches Hyder Ali the way a dispossessed man watches his old house's lit windows.

{=!}Abdullah Khan of Barha, elder of the Sayyid Brothers, is the kingmaker of Hindostan. The Sayyids of Barha have bled for the Mughal throne for two centuries, always first into the breach; Abdullah has converted that legacy of blood into the power to crown and uncrown emperors. It was his soldiers and his brother Hussain Ali who raised Farrukhsiyar, and his patience that unmade him. The wazir's title sits on him like armour. Every Turani noble at court — Qamar ud Din Khan above all — prays daily for his fall, and Abdullah knows it, and dares them.

{=!}Hussain Ali Khan of Barha, the younger Sayyid Brother, is the sword where Abdullah Khan is the pen. First Bakhshi of the empire and the finest battlefield commander at court, he marched an army to the Deccan and came back with a treaty with the Marathas that scandalised Delhi — and secured his family's grip. Hot where his brother is cold, he has never forgiven an insult in his life. Half the daggers in Hindostan are said to be reserved for him.

{=!}Mirza Najaf Khan traces his blood to the Safavid kings of Persia, and carries himself accordingly. He came east with little but a sword and a lineage, and has made himself the most dependable soldier the Peacock Throne still commands — patient, methodical, incorruptible by the standards of a court where everything is for sale. He despises the Sayyid Brothers as jumped-up praetorians and makes little secret of it; Abdullah Khan Barha, in turn, has twice blocked his advancement. The emperor trusts him precisely because no faction owns him.

{=!}Ali Muhammad Khan was born nothing — an adopted orphan raised among the Rohilla Afghans of Katehr — and has made himself master of a country. Where the empire's grip slackened north of the Ganga, he gathered the Rohilla warbands, fortified the towns, and collected the revenue the court could not. Delhi calls him a usurper; the peasants call him a better landlord than the last three imperial governors. His feud with Muhammad Khan Bangash, who covets the same country, is the steadiest war on the doab's frontier.

{=!}Muhammad Khan Bangash sold his sword up and down Hindostan for thirty years — a true soldier of fortune of the Afghan school — before the throne made him a nawab for it. Blunt, brave, and loyal to whoever pays on time, he is the emperor's favourite hammer, and Delhi points him wherever the ground shakes. His rivalry with Ali Muhammad Khan of the Rohillas is personal as much as territorial: two self-made Afghans, one prize. He calls the court's silk-robed factions 'the embroidery' and is tolerated because he wins.

{=!}Amir Khan of the Khas-Khel is the perfect courtier of a declining court: exquisite manners, exquisite debts, and an unerring sense of which way a room is about to turn. He attached his house early to the Turani party of Qamar ud Din Khan and has prospered by it. The wits of Shahjahanabad say Amir Khan has never drawn his sword; the wise reply that he has never needed to.

{=!}Qamar ud Din Khan is the head of the Turani party — the old Central Asian military nobility that considers the empire its family property — and cousin to Asaf Jah of the Deccan, a connection he wears like a jewel. Measured, immensely dignified, and slow to move until the moment is certain, he regards the Sayyid Brothers of Barha as a disease of the state and waits for the fever to break. When it does, the wazirate his father held will come home to his house — he has planned for nothing else.

{=!}Ibrahim Khan, styled Firuz Jung, commands what remains of the Qizilbash — the Persian mounted gentry that once formed the empire's steel spine. His grandfather's generation conquered the Deccan under Alamgir; his own inherits the debts of that war. A professional to his fingertips, he holds the Qizilbash apart from the court's Sayyid-Turani duel, remarking that a soldier who chooses a faction has chosen his executioner. Najaf Khan, a fellow Persian, is the nearest thing he admits to a friend.

{=!}Sadullah Khan of the Badakshi house descends from émigré scholars of the high Oxus valleys who came south for Mughal patronage and stayed to serve four emperors. His family produces qazis, revenue men, and the occasional excellent general, and prides itself on clean hands in a dirty age. He keeps the friendship of the scholar-house of Bilgram in the far Deccan — letters travel between them monthly — and the registers of half the doab travel across his desk.

{=!}Fateh Chand, titled Jagat Seth — 'Banker of the World' — and the title is scarcely an exaggeration. From his counting-houses in Murshidabad his family finances the revenue of Bengal, the trade of the European companies, and, when it suits him, the ambitions of emperors: the exchange rate of the rupee moves when he clears his throat. He and Murshid Quli Khan built Bengal's prosperity as a partnership of ledger and law, and neither pretends the province could run without the other. Armies borrow; the Jagat Seth collects.

{=!}Mir Jumla came to Bengal as the court's man — sent east with imperial letters and the late Farrukhsiyar's favour to watch the too-independent diwan. He stayed to become part of what he was sent to watch. A polished courtier with a genuine gift for the law, he sits uneasily between Delhi's demands and Murshid Quli Khan's cold competence, and has made himself useful to both rather than owned by either. The zamindars call him 'the weathervane'; he considers it a compliment to his instruments.

{=!}Jafar Khan holds Purnea, the eastern march of Bengal, where the revenue country runs out into hills that have never seen a tax collector twice. Half his work is war against raiders, half is the quieter war of keeping his frontier jagir from being reassessed by Murshid Quli Khan's relentless surveyors. A hard, capable, unglamorous man — Bengal's soft wealth is guarded by his entirely unsoft frontier.

{=!}Reza Khan farms the revenue of the Nadia country, the rich rice heartland where Bengal's wealth is actually grown. He is the diwan's ideal servant: exact, loyal, and colourless, delivering the assessment to the day. Beneath the exactness is an ambition his master has perhaps not fully priced — Reza Khan has watched how a diwan's office can become a throne, and takes careful notes.

{=!}Kirtichand Roy of the Burdwan Raj heads the greatest of the Hindu zamindari houses on which Murshid Quli Khan's whole revenue settlement stands. His grandfather held a market town; Kirtichand holds a country, expanded estate by estate through loans, lawsuits, and the diwan's favour. The house of Burdwan pays magnificently and punctually, and in exchange is left to rule its tenants like a kingdom within the kingdom — an arrangement both sides find too profitable to disturb.

{=!}Haibat Jung holds the Rajshahi country, vast estates north of the Ganga that came to his house when Murshid Quli Khan broke the old defaulting zamindars and needed steadier hands. The title — 'Terror in War' — was won honestly against the hill raiders, but his real weapon is the survey: he knows every bigha of his lands and exactly what it owes. Kirtichand Roy of Burdwan is his rival in everything: acreage, precedence at the diwan's darbar, and the marriage market.

{=!}Azim ush Shan of the Dacca nawabs carries imperial blood and has never once let anyone forget it. His house descends from the prince who governed Bengal before Murshid Quli Khan's rise, and it regards the diwan — a converted revenue clerk, as they never tire of saying — as a usurper in their family's province. From old Dacca they maintain a shadow court, more splendid than solvent, and wait for Delhi to remember its own. Murshid Quli taxes their estates with particular, unhurried care.

{=!}Rayan ud Daula of the Dinajpur Raj rules the northern rice country where Bengal shades into the Himalayan approaches. His house made its fortune provisioning armies it prudently declined to join, and its granaries have decided more than one campaign season. Cautious to a fault, he pays Murshid Quli Khan's assessment early, banks with the Jagat Seth, and has never in his life stood within a mile of a battle. His neighbours mock him and borrow from him in the same breath.

{=!}Ikhtiyar ul Mulk heads the Paigah, the household nobility that serves as the sword-arm of the Asaf Jahi court — bound to the Nizam's family by marriage in every generation and by loyalty older than that. The Paigah maintain their own cavalry at their own cost, point of pride and instrument of policy both. Where Asaf Jah calculates, the Paigah simply ride; the combination has kept the Deccan's enemies guessing for a generation. Against the Marathas of Bajirao he counsels the attack, always, and is always overruled — so far.

{=!}Rustam Dil Khan of the Salar Jung house belongs to the Deccan's ministerial aristocracy — the families that administer while the Paigah fight and the Nizam thinks. Persian in culture, exact in the revenue, and dry as a ledger in conversation, he is the man Asaf Jah trusts with the arithmetic of the state. He considers Bajirao's Maratha confederacy a tax problem that has been allowed to become a war, and can prove it with figures.

{=!}Mukarram ud Daula holds Kalyani and the western marches of the Nizamat — the country that burns first whenever the Maratha horse comes over the ghats. His fortresses are strong, his levies experienced, and his patience with Hyderabad's courtly deliberations extremely thin. He has paid the chauth to Maratha bands with one hand and hanged their collectors with the other, as the season required, and sleeps soundly. Wali Shah of Berar, who shares his frontier, shares his temper.

{=!}Saadatullah Khan, Nawab of Arcot, governs the Carnatic — the rich temple country of the far south — with a courtesy so perfect it takes visitors a while to notice he obeys nobody. Nominally under the Deccan's authority, he corresponds with Asaf Jah as one sovereign to another, taxes the European trading posts on his coast with connoisseurship, and has made Arcot a state in everything but proclamation. The Nizam's court files his letters under 'diplomacy', which is itself a concession.

{=!}Yaqut Khan commands the Habshi of Janjira — the African-descended admiralty that has held its island fortress against every power on the western coast for a century and a half. The sea is his estate: convoy fees, escort contracts, and the imperial title of admiral that Alamgir's house confirmed and no one since has dared revoke. His war with the Angre — Laxmibai's Maratha corsairs down the coast — is the longest-running naval feud in Hindostan, conducted with professional thoroughness and genuine relish on both sides.

{=!}Salabat Khan's house are Deccani soldiers of the old sultanate stock — the families that served Bijapur and Golconda before Alamgir broke them, and then served the conqueror with the same competence. Twice burned by history, the Salabat Khani keep their loyalty professional and their options open. He drills his cavalry on European lines he half-invented himself, and both Asaf Jah and the Marathas have found it cheaper to hire him than to fight him.

{=!}Wali Shah holds Berar, the cotton-rich plain that is less a province than a permanent negotiation: Raghuji Bhonsle of Nagpur claims half its revenue by treaty, custom, and cheerful armed insistence. Wali Shah pays what he must, fortifies what he can, and prides himself on knowing to the rupee the difference between tribute and robbery. He and Mukarram ud Daula of Kalyani hold the Nizamat's whole western wall between them, and drink to their thankless arithmetic when they meet.

{=!}Habib ur Rahman of the Bilgrami house belongs to a family famous across Hindostan for producing scholars the way other houses produce soldiers — poets, jurists, chroniclers, and the occasional formidably learned administrator. He serves the Deccan as chief of its chancery, and his letters are collected and imitated as models of Persian prose. His correspondence with Sadullah Khan of the Badakshi house in Delhi is one of the last threads still stitching the empire's two halves together.

{=!}Kanthirava Narasaraja carries the name of Mysore's greatest warrior-king and the burden of being a royal cadet: close enough to the Gaddi to matter, far enough to be dangerous. His house of Bettada Kote guards the Wodeyar dynasty's older traditions — temple patronage, wrestling grounds, the Dasara festival kept in full state — against the new men of the army. He is loyal to Krishnaraja Wodeyar without reservation, and to the Dalvai commanders like Hyder Ali with very many reservations indeed.

{=!}Basappaji Nayaka of Maddur descends from the Nayaka captains who ruled the southern country between empires — soldier-farmers who fortified every hilltop and forgot none of them. His levies are the steadiest infantry Mysore fields, and he knows it, and prices it. He took Hyder Ali's measure early, when the future Dalvai was a mere trooper, and backed him; the friendship of the two self-made soldiers is now a load-bearing wall of the state.

{=!}Mana Vikrama bears the ancient title of Zamorin of Calicut, lord of the Malabar pepper coast where the wealth of three continents changes hands on his wharves. His house has traded with, taxed, and periodically fought every seafaring nation on earth for four hundred years and is impressed by none of them. His hereditary quarrel with the Raja of Palakkad — Kombi Achan, who guards the one good pass through the ghats — is older than most kingdoms, and Kombi Achan's flirtations with Mysore make it sharper every year.

{=!}Dodda Vira Rajendra of the Haleri line rules Coorg, the coffee-green mountain country whose warriors are as steep and unforgiving as its passes. Coorg has never been conquered — a fact recited at his court roughly hourly — and he intends to keep the record intact against precisely one threat: the growing power of Mysore's army and its Dalvai, Hyder Ali. Mountain, musket, and memory are his three fortresses. He watches the roads up from Srirangapatna the way other men watch the monsoon.

{=!}Hayat Quli Khan holds Sira, the old Mughal faujdari of the south — the office through which Alamgir's empire once governed everything below the Krishna, now an island of imperial ceremony in a Mysorean sea. His house keeps the forms with immaculate stubbornness: the khutba read, the offices staffed, the correspondence with distant Delhi maintained. The house of Sira's old feud with the Kalale — begun over a slain hero and a wounded pride in the last generation — still poisons the south's politics, and Hyder Ali of the Kalale connection has not forgotten it either.

{=!}Kombi Achan, Raja of Palakkad, holds the great gap in the western ghats — the only road by which armies pass between the Carnatic and the Malabar coast — and has built a statecraft entirely on being in the way. Squeezed for generations by the Zamorin of Calicut's greater wealth and older title, his house has learned to survive by inviting in whichever larger power annoys the Zamorin most; of late his letters go to Mysore, to Hyder Ali, who reads them with great interest. A small king with a large door.

{=!}Ajit Singh Rathore of Marwar spent his entire childhood as a fugitive from Alamgir's court — smuggled out of Delhi as an infant while his father's kingdom was seized — and won it all back by outliving the emperor. That long war made him proud, suspicious, and immovable: he bends to Delhi when it suits Marwar and defies it when it doesn't, sometimes in the same season. His alliance with Sawai Jai Singh of Amber, sealed by marriage, is real but rivalrous — the two greatest Rajput kings agree on everything except which of them is greater.

{=!}Hadi Rani of the Sisodia holds Mewar, the proudest throne in Rajputana — the house that never gave a daughter to the Mughals and never lets the others forget it. Widowed young and never remarried, she rules in her son's name with a severity the chronicles will call legendary: it is said she once sent a wavering commander her own ultimatum wrapped in a silk thread from her wedding dress. Mewar's precedence over Amber and Marwar is not negotiable at her darbar; Jai Singh and Ajit Singh handle her the way men handle fire.

{=!}Budh Singh Hada of Bundi is brother-in-law to Sawai Jai Singh — a marriage that was meant to bind the Hada country to Amber and has instead bound Budh Singh to a lifetime of watching his powerful kinsman's ambitions with one eye open. A blunt, generous, hard-drinking soldier of the old Rajput school, he is happiest in the saddle and worst at court, where the Kachwaha diplomacy runs rings around him. His loyalty, once given, does not move; Jai Singh trades on that, and Budh Singh knows it, and gives it anyway.

{=!}Swarup Singh Bhati rules Jaisalmer, the golden fortress-city in the deep desert where the caravan roads to Sind and Persia cross. The Bhati have outlasted every empire by the simple expedient of being too far away and too poor in water to be worth conquering — and too rich in transit tolls to be ignored. He gives the desert's hospitality with royal grace and taxes it with royal thoroughness. The Banjara caravans call him 'the gatekeeper'; he has been called worse by better.

{=!}Anand Singh Shekhawat leads the Shekhawat brotherhood — the warlike cadet-kin of the Kachwaha who hold the dry country north of Amber in a lattice of little forts and long memories. The Shekhawats fight for Jai Singh as family, which is to say: fiercely, and with a running commentary of grievances about pay and precedence. Anand Singh has refused better offers from Delhi twice, a fact he mentions whenever Amber's treasurer is within earshot.

{=!}Prithviraj Chauhan carries the most storied name in Rajput history, and his house has spent five centuries both blessed and crushed by it. The Chauhans of today hold a modest country and an immodest legacy — every darbar they enter, the bards recite the last stand against the Ghurids, and every treaty they sign is measured against ancestors who signed none. Prithviraj bears it with a dry humour his ancestors would have deplored. In the field, however, the name still does half his recruiting for him.

{=!}Ratan Singh Jhala comes of the house whose ancestor took the royal insignia onto his own body at Khanwa so the Rana could escape the field — the most famous act of vassal loyalty in Rajput memory. The Jhalas have been trading on it, and living up to it, ever since. Ratan Singh serves as Mewar's shield on the western approaches and Hadi Rani's most trusted commander; she, who trusts almost no one, is said to trust him entirely. He has never given her cause to revise the arrangement.

{=!}Ram Singh Tanwar descends from the kings who ruled Delhi before the sultans came — a fact his house has carried through seven centuries of other people's empires like a deed to a property everyone else has built on. The Tanwars hold their small country with unremarkable competence and remarkable patience. Ram Singh's toast, given at every wedding and understood by every Rajput present, is simply: 'To the long game.'

{=!}Jai Singh Gaur leads a house that chose, generations ago, the unfashionable Rajput specialty of holding the eastern approaches — the low, rich, indefensible country where Rajputana shades into the imperial doab. The Gaurs learned to fight delaying actions, to fortify grain rather than glory, and to negotiate from inside a siege. Delhi's armies, Maratha bands, and freebooting Afghans have all crossed his country; remarkably few have crossed it twice by the same road.

{=!}Vikramaditya Baghel rules the forest country of the east, where Rajputana's politics grow distant and the tiger is a more regular petitioner than the tax collector. The Baghels are the furthest-flung of the great Rajput houses and cultivate the independence of the far-flung: they attend the councils of Jai Singh and Ajit Singh late, leave early, and agree to nothing binding. His hillmen archers and his knowledge of the forest tracks make him a bad enemy and — as the Bundelkhand rebels have found — a profitable friend.

{=!}Shahu Bhonsle, grandson of the great Shivaji, is Chhatrapati — and spent eighteen years of his childhood a hostage in Alamgir's camp, learning the empire from inside its tent. That captivity made him the strangest of Maratha kings: courteous, unhurried, forgiving, and quietly unbreakable. He rules by choosing servants greater than himself and trusting them utterly; his Peshwa, Bajirao, wields powers no minister has held before, because Shahu decided genius should not wait on protocol. The old sardars grumble that the servant has become the state. Shahu smiles, and lets them.

{=!}Ranoji Scindia began as the Peshwa's slipper-bearer — the story is told in every Maratha camp, usually by Ranoji himself, laughing — and rose by pure ability to command the confederacy's vanguard on the Malwa roads. He is Bajirao's creature and proud of it: first over the Narmada, first into the cannon-smoke, first named in the dispatches. With Malhar Rao Holkar he keeps a running rivalry of raids, races, and outrageous wagers that has cost both fortunes and won the confederacy half of Malwa.

{=!}Malhar Rao Holkar was a shepherd boy on the Deccan plateau; he is now one of the great cavalry commanders of the age, and has never learned to read anything but ground and men — the two subjects that matter. Bajirao spotted him leading a grain-guard skirmish and gave him a banner on the spot. His partnership-rivalry with Ranoji Scindia is the confederacy's twin engine: where one raids, the other must raid farther. Blunt, superstitious, openhanded, and utterly without pretension, he is the army's favourite of all its generals.

{=!}Pilaji Gaekwad built the Maratha power in Gujarat raid by raid, and holds it in the name of the Senapati's faction — the old military party that watched the young Peshwa Bajirao gather the confederacy's reins into Brahmin hands and has never accepted it. The quarrel is not abstract: Gujarat's tribute is claimed by both the Senapati's office and the Peshwa's, and Pilaji collects it first and argues afterwards. Men who know both camps say the confederacy's first civil war is already written; only the date is blank.

{=!}Raghuji Bhonsle of Nagpur is the Chhatrapati's kinsman and never lets the Peshwa forget it. From Nagpur his horse command the eastern roads — Berar's cotton, the tribute of the forest rajas, and the long road toward Bengal's fabled treasury, which he speaks of the way other men speak of a betrothed. He acknowledges Shahu with real devotion and Bajirao's office with none at all: to Raghuji, the Peshwa is a minister who forgot himself. The east, he says, needs no permission from Pune.

{=!}Parashuram Patwardhan heads a Brahmin house that serves the Peshwa's cause with a devotion the older sardars find slightly indecent: bankers, priests, and — to everyone's initial surprise — very good soldiers. The Patwardhans hold the southern marches against Mysore and the Nizamat, run remounts and revenue for Pune, and tie their fortunes wholly to Bajirao's. If the Peshwa's star falls, theirs falls with it; Parashuram has done the arithmetic and bet the house anyway.

{=!}Laxmibai Angre rules the Angre admiralty from the harbour-fortress of Vijaydurg: the corsair state her father Kanhoji built, which taxes every sail on the Konkan coast and answers, in strict truth, to nobody. She holds the Chhatrapati's commission and interprets it broadly; the Peshwa's polite requests for coordination are received with equally polite silence. Her standing war with Yaqut Khan's Sidi fleet of Janjira is the coast's oldest institution. European captains pay her passes, curse her name, and privately concede the passes are cheaper than the alternative.

{=!}Darshan Kaur leads the Ahluwalia misl as its widow-matriarch — her husband fell in the great persecution, and the sardars who expected her to retire to grief discovered instead the sharpest political mind in the Panth. She keeps the misl's treasury, its marriages, and its feuds with equal precision, and stands at the centre of every effort to make the quarrelling misls act as one Dal. Her long duel with Jassa Singh Ramgarhia — over forts, precedence, and one never-forgiven insult at Amritsar — is conducted in perfect courtesy and total earnest.

{=!}Jassa Singh Ramgarhia is the Panth's master of fortification — a carpenter's son who learned siegecraft by enduring sieges, and who rebuilt the fort of Ram Rauni with his own hands when the misls despaired of holding it. What he builds, he keeps: forts, guns, grudges. His feud with Darshan Kaur's Ahluwalia misl divides gatherings that should stand united, and both know it, and neither will yield first. Against the Afghan invasions, though, his walls belong to everyone — even her.

{=!}Jai Singh Singhpuria inherited his misl from the revered Nawab Kapur Singh's line, and with it a moral authority in the Panth that his modest lands do not explain. When the misl sardars deadlock, it is the Singhpuria voice that is asked to speak first; when the Dal Khalsa musters, his banner rides at the honoured place. He spends the inheritance carefully — mediating the Ahluwalia-Ramgarhia feud, restraining the young Sukerchakia, and reminding all of them, at useful moments, whose approval they still crave.

{=!}Gulab Singh Dallewalia commands the misl of the river crossings, whose riders make their living on the Beas and Sutlej fords — tolls in peacetime, ambushes in war. The Dallewalia are the Dal Khalsa's scouts and skirmishers by custom, first to see every Afghan army that comes down from the passes and first to bring the word. Gulab Singh himself is a spare, silent man who has personally counted every invasion of his lifetime from a hilltop, and regards the number as unfinished business.

{=!}Hira Singh Nakai leads the misl of the Nakka country, the hard scrubland south of Lahore between the rivers. The Nakai are farmers who fight and fighters who farm, unglamorous and unbreakable, and their sardar matches the country: plain-spoken, devout, and permanently unimpressed. His riders answered the old Padishah's silver once, in the Deccan war, and came home with pay, scars, and a settled conviction that empires are poor employers. Mai Bhago's memory is kept with special honour at his hearth.

{=!}Jai Singh Kanhaiya built his misl in the rich country north of Amritsar with a boldness that shades, his rivals say, into appetite. He and young Charat Singh Sukerchakia began as comrades in the same jatha; the friendship has curdled into the Panth's most dangerous rivalry, two ambitious houses expanding toward the same rivers, the same tolls, the same future. Jai Singh is the elder, the richer, and — he would add — the wiser. The Sukerchakia bloodline, men whisper, may nonetheless have the longer star.

{=!}Ala Singh of the Phulkian house rules at Patiala, southernmost of the great Sikh chiefs, where the Panth's country meets Delhi's — and he negotiates with both, in both directions, without embarrassment. He has taken imperial titles when they were useful and Khalsa vows that are entirely sincere, and sees no contradiction: the house survives, the faith endures, the wheat gets in. The northern misls mistrust him for it; Jai Singh Kanhaiya calls him 'the Padishah's Sikh' — but when the Afghans come down the passes, Ala Singh's grain and gold reach the Dal Khalsa first.

{=!}Karora Singh leads the misl that bears his name in the broken country toward the hills, a sardar of the old irregular school: strike, vanish, endure. His band began as refugees of the great persecution and keeps a refugee's memory — no misl gives shelter more readily, and none forgives the empire's old cruelties less. In the Dal Khalsa's councils he speaks seldom and votes always for the raid. The young riders love him; the treasurers of the plains towns have his description posted.

{=!}Payinda Khan leads the Barakzai, the largest and hungriest of the Durrani tribes — the house that furnishes Ahmad Shah's armies with their steadiest regiments and watches the Sadozai throne with the patience of a creditor. The Barakzai serve loyally, fight superbly, and remember precisely; every honour granted to a Popalzai kinsman is entered in their private ledger of the future. Payinda Khan himself is gracious, generous, and — in the judgment of every rival who has studied him — the most dangerous man in the Afghan kingdom after the Shah he serves.

{=!}Suleiman Shah of the Popalzai is Ahmad Shah Durrani's kinsman-tribe and his throne's inner keep: the Popalzai provide the court, the bodyguard, and the unquestioned loyalty on which everything else is built. Suleiman Shah wears the role with the easy confidence of a man whose family's blood is the dynasty's blood. He counts Payinda Khan Barakzai a brother-in-arms and watches him like a rival heir — both attitudes entirely sincere, in the Afghan way.

{=!}Ali Khan leads the Alakozai, the tribe of the rich Arghandab orchards, whose horsemen are reckoned the finest heavy cavalry the Durrani confederacy fields. The Alakozai pride themselves on being kingmakers who never claim the crown: they backed Ahmad Shah's election at the Sher Surkh jirga and expect that debt to be serviced in perpetuity, in offices, commands, and first pick of the spoils of Hindostan. Ali Khan collects with courtesy and a very long memory.

{=!}Shahpasand Khan of the Achakzai commands the confederacy's outriders — the tribe Ahmad Shah trusts to screen his marches, raid ahead of his columns, and hold the bleak country where Kandahar's writ thins into desert. The Achakzai were carved from the Barakzai generations ago and cherish the separation; Payinda Khan's house they treat as kin at weddings and competitors everywhere else. Shahpasand Khan earned his name — 'pleasing to the king' — at the head of a charge, and has been living up to it, expensively, ever since.

{=!}Mir Mahmud Hotak carries the terrible inheritance of the Ghilzai: his kinsman Mir Wais freed Kandahar from the Persians and his house then conquered Isfahan itself — an empire won and lost within a generation. The Ghilzai have not forgotten being kings, and have not forgiven the Abdali tribes — Ahmad Shah's Sadozai above all — for inheriting the world the Hotaks broke open. Mir Mahmud serves the Durrani confederacy when it marches on Hindostan and plots against it the rest of the year, roughly on schedule, and everyone including Ahmad Shah knows it.

{=!}Khushal Khan of the Afridi holds the Khyber — the pass through which every conqueror of Hindostan has marched, paid, or died — and his tribe has taken its toll from all three kinds. Named for the great warrior-poet of his people, he keeps up the family tradition in both trades: his verses are quoted around the fires, and his tolls are quoted, with feeling, by every caravan master from Kabul to Lahore. Kings hire the Khyber; nobody owns it. Ahmad Shah, who understands mountains, pays promptly.

{=!}Mir Ali Khan speaks for the Yusufzai, the great valley-holding tribe of the northern hills — too numerous to conquer, too proud to unite, too well-armed to tax. The Yusufzai jirgas gave Alamgir's empire its bloodiest frontier lesson generations ago and retell it at length. Mir Ali Khan brings his lashkars to the Durrani muster as an ally, never a subject, a distinction he is prepared to explain with rifles. Ahmad Shah, who could conquer Delhi sooner than the Yusufzai valleys, prefers the alliance.

{=!}Nur Muhammad of the Ishakzai leads the westernmost of the Durrani tribes, the herding lords of the Helmand margins whose camels move the confederacy's baggage and whose horsemen guard its rear. The Ishakzai are the quiet partners of the Durrani compact — seldom at the head of the darbar, never absent from the campaign roll. Nur Muhammad's own ambitions are bounded and exact: grazing rights, a fair share of Hindostan's plunder, and precedence, someday, over the Alakozai. Two of the three, he likes to say, are in hand.
